topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the schematic spread across the battered oak table, her jaw tightening until her molars ached. The intricate wiring diagrams, the precise measurements for the kinetic dampeners—it was all exactly the same as the ones she’d been wrestling with for the last three weeks. A cold knot formed in her stomach, spreading outward like ice water. She picked up a stylus, her hand trembling slightly, and traced a line on the blueprint. The ink felt alien, mocking the labor she’d poured into it. When she glanced across the cluttered workshop, she saw Marcus hunched over a nearly identical assembly, his brow furrowed in concentration. He looked up, catching her gaze, and a flicker of something—recognition? annoyance?—crossed his face. Elara quickly dropped the stylus, the small clatter sounding deafening in the silence. She pushed back from the table, the movement sharp and jerky, needing distance from the suffocating similarity.

[story 2]

"No. No, this can't be right," thought Ben, his breath catching in his throat as he compared the prototype casing in his hands to the finalized design printed on the monitor. Every curve, every port placement, every micro-screw placement was an exact mirror image of the project he’d spent sleepless nights perfecting. A wave of profound unease washed over him, making his palms sweat. He leaned back from the workbench, the cheap metal vibrating faintly under his touch. Across the room, Liam was meticulously polishing a component that matched Ben’s own in every conceivable way. Ben swallowed hard, his throat suddenly dry. He couldn't look at Liam; the sheer, unsettling perfection of the duplication made him want to physically recoil. He began frantically gathering his tools, the clinking of metal against metal sounding frantic and desperate.

[story 3]

"Are you serious?" Sarah whispered, the sound thin and brittle in the cavernous studio. She held up the data slate, the complex algorithms scrolling across the screen—the exact same sequence of calculations she had painstakingly derived. It was as if someone had stolen her intellectual journey and handed it directly to another person. A tightness constricted her chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. She felt a profound sense of violation, as if her own creative process had been duplicated, stolen, and then handed back to her in a horrifyingly familiar form. When she turned, she saw David across the room, his posture mirroring her own tension, staring intently at a device that was unmistakably hers. Sarah took a step back, her eyes wide and fixed on the uncanny resemblance.
